# Chapter 06 - Chapter 6

## Draft Paragraphs (keyed)

[[CH06-P0010]] Forty-seven minutes. The same duration as the day she first arrived, only this time the number ticked in her head like a warning. Marnette sat in the back corner and watched the salve district’s tight grey corridors give way to the transit tubes, then to the neutral zones with their wider walkways and brighter light. She kept her breathing slow and her face blank, practising the calm she was meant to wear. She had rehearsed the story through a sleepless night. She had left something behind at Residence 7B, a personal item, her mother’s carving of Elom. It was plausible. Caregivers forgot small things in the aftermath of a death. The carving was real, too, which was the cruel part. It sat safe on her bedside table beside its copy, and she was about to use it as a key. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0020]] The lie sat in Marnette’s chest like a swallowed stone. She had never been good at deception. Her face betrayed her, her voice slipped on the wrong syllables. Eight years of caregiving had trained her into honesty, not performance, into comfort that came from being present rather than pretending. Now she was heading back into a Master’s residence under false pretences. She would be looking for proof that could tear the colony’s order open, and every instinct she had been taught about duty and obedience was already braced to call it betrayal. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0030]] It was necessary. It was the only way. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0040]] Morning made the Master district feel almost subdued. The corridors were quieter than she remembered, the usual traffic of household staff and visitors thinned by the mourning protocols. Black banners hung at measured intervals, grief standardised into decoration. At every junction memorial displays carried Frost’s face, always the same sanctioned images. The scholar. The historian. A Master who had lived nearly eight centuries and was now being folded into the colony’s story as neatly as a paragraph in a textbook. Marnette kept her eyes on the floor and still could not avoid him. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0050]] She did not look at the images. She could not. Every time Frost’s face caught the edge of her vision, she heard his last directive as if it were spoken into her ear, not memory but command. The Archive. Find the truth. They lied. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0060]] Residence 7B waited at the end of the corridor, its ornate door closed but not sealed. In mourning, the residence was maintained and left empty, preserved like a shrine until the year ran out and the space could be claimed again. Black banners framed the entrance. Beside the door sat a small arrangement of artificial flowers, immaculate and scentless, grief made safe for display. Marnette slowed as she approached, aware of how exposed she was in the open corridor, how easily a simple errand could become a question. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0070]] Marnette approached with her heart racing and her palms damp against her grey tunic. She had rehearsed this part too. Knock. Introduce herself. Ask permission to retrieve a forgotten personal item. A caregiver’s small mistake, corrected with humility. She was still a step away from the panel when the door opened. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0080]] Elara stood in the doorway, silver-threaded hair pulled back from a face that seemed to have aged in the last six weeks. Her eyes were rimmed red and her posture had a new bend to it, weariness that reached deeper than lack of sleep. She had served Frost for decades. She had watched him thin and fail, managed his affairs, and kept her understanding locked behind professional stillness. Seeing her now, Marnette felt the weight of all the things Elara must have held without speaking. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0090]] "Marnette." Elara’s voice held no surprise. "I wondered when you would come." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0100]] The lie she had rehearsed went dry in Marnette’s mouth. "You knew?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0110]] "I knew you were different when you left," Elara said. "I saw it in your eyes the day Master Frost died. You were looking at everything as if it had changed shape." She stepped aside and made room for Marnette to enter. "He chose you for a reason. He told you things he shouldn’t have. He planted what he wanted planted." Her gaze held steady. "I’ve been waiting to see whether it would take." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0120]] Marnette stepped inside and the air hit her, expensive antiseptic threaded with old paper. The residence felt hollowed out. Frost had filled these rooms even when he could barely lift his head; now there was only silence, absence made physical. Through the open door of his chamber she saw what had been removed. No equipment. No monitors. The bed stripped down to bare order, as if wiping surfaces could erase what had happened here. Only the furniture remained, and the way her own memory insisted on placing him back into it. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0130]] "Why are you helping me?" Marnette asked as Elara closed the door behind them. "You could report me. You could pretend you don’t know why I’m here." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0140]] "Because Master Frost wanted someone to know," Elara said. "He spent his last weeks trying to get the truth into your hands, even when his mouth failed him." Her eyes met Marnette’s and Marnette saw something beneath the grief, something older. Guilt, set deep. "I’ve known for thirty-seven years," Elara said quietly. "Thirty-seven years of keeping it to myself because silence was easier than facing what it meant. Master Frost was right about one thing. The truth does not die when we refuse to name it. It waits." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0150]] Thirty-seven years. Elara had carried it longer than Marnette had been alive, moving through her duties with that knowledge sealed inside her, watching salves live and die without ever hearing why. She had served in this residence, cared for a man who had helped build the system that measured out her own years, and kept the surface smooth with every prayer repeated and every task completed on time. The endurance of it made Marnette’s stomach turn. So did the familiarity. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0160]] "How do you live with it?" Marnette whispered. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0170]] "You find ways," Elara said. "You tell yourself it is not your place to disrupt the order. You tell yourself that knowing changes nothing, that the truth would only add pain without offering remedy. You tell yourself one person cannot shift a system that has stood for seven centuries." Her voice tightened, a thin crack in the composure she wore like armour. "You learn to survive by making smaller lies around the larger one. Then a dying man looks at you with centuries of regret and asks you to help set something right. Then you realise what your silence has done. It has not protected anyone. It has only kept the lie alive." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0180]] Elara led her down the corridor to the far door, the one that opened into the Archive. Their footsteps barely sounded on the polished floor, yet Marnette felt each one as if the house itself were listening. The silence pressed in, not calm but concentrated, as though the residence had become a sealed vessel and any wrong word might crack it. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0190]] "You have perhaps two hours," Elara said. "Maintenance comes each morning to inspect the residence. Climate, seals, protocol. They will not enter the Archive. Frost’s collections are still being catalogued by the historical society." Her glance cut to Marnette. "But they will notice an extra person where there should be none." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0200]] "Two hours," Marnette repeated, and felt the time tighten around her ribs. "How am I supposed to find anything in two hours. The Archive is enormous. I don’t even know what I’m looking for." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0210]] "Master Frost left notes," Elara said. "Research he compiled over the last decade, trying to piece together what was done and why. He knew he was dying, Marnette. He had time to prepare. He organised what he wanted found." She stopped at the Archive door with her hand poised over the panel. "Look for a folder marked Mayfly. That was his name for it, the thing that never left him. The basics are there." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0220]] "And you?" Marnette asked. "Will you come with me?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0230]] Elara shook her head. "I have seen enough. I have known enough for thirty-seven years, and the knowing has been its own prison." Her gaze held on Marnette with a tired steadiness. "What you find in there will change you. It will strip away the person you were and leave you to build someone else. I do not know if I have enough life left to become someone new again." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0240]] Elara pressed her palm to the panel and the Archive door opened. Beyond it waited the room that had been pulling at Marnette for weeks, the place where truth had been stored away like contraband. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0250]] "Be careful," Elara said. "When you leave, do not come back here. Whatever you find, whatever you decide to do with it, this residence cannot be part of it. Too many eyes. Too many questions." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0260]] Marnette nodded, drew one steadying breath, and stepped into the Archive. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0270]] *** <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0280]] The room swallowed her. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0290]] Marnette had stood in this room once, on the night of Frost’s crisis, when he sent her in for a single book. She had taken it and fled, overwhelmed by what she could not afford to notice. Now she stood alone as the door slid shut behind her, and there was no errand to hide inside. The full scope of what Frost had gathered over nearly eight centuries bore down on her, not as awe but as obligation. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH06-P0300]] The Archive was larger than her entire living quarters, with walls lined floor-to-ceiling with shelves that groaned under books, scrolls, data crystals, and artifacts she couldn't identify. Multiple data screens glowed softly around the room, displaying information that scrolled automatically—star charts, genetic sequences, historical timelines, documents in languages she didn't recognize. The air smelled of old paper and leather, of dust that had settled over centuries, of the metallic tang of ancient electronics struggling to preserve data that had been recorded before her grandmother was born.

[[CH06-P0310]] Where was she supposed to even begin?

[[CH06-P0320]] Mayfly. Elara had said to look for a folder marked Mayfly. Project Mayfly—the phrase she had glimpsed in a book during her first visit, the words that had haunted her dreams ever since. Optimized 50-year lifecycle parameters.

[[CH06-P0330]] Marnette moved deeper into the room, her footsteps muffled by the worn carpet. The organization system, if there was one, made no sense to her untrained eyes. Books were arranged not by subject or date but by some personal logic that had existed only in Frost's mind—perhaps by when he had acquired them, or by their significance to his research, or simply by whatever shelf had space at the moment. Data crystals were grouped in clusters that seemed random: historical records beside genetic research, colony charters next to personal correspondence, technical manuals mixed with what appeared to be fiction from Earth.

[[CH06-P0340]] She started with the center table, where the book with the brown leather cover still sat exactly as she had left it six weeks ago. The book fell open to the same marked page she had seen before: "Project Mayfly - Initial Implementation Phase." Below it, the same terrible words: "Subject Population: 85% of colonists designated as biological workforce with optimized 50-year lifecycle parameters."

[[CH06-P0350]] But there was more on the page than she had noticed before, more than she had been willing to see when she was still clinging to the hope that Frost's words were the ravings of a dying mind. Her eyes, now searching for meaning rather than fleeing from it, caught additional phrases:

[[CH06-P0360]] "Phase 1: Genetic modification of embryonic stem cells to implement predetermined cellular degradation sequences."

[[CH06-P0370]] "Phase 2: Implementation of apoptosis triggers calibrated to initiate systemic organ failure within specified timeline parameters."

[[CH06-P0380]] "Phase 3: Establishment of religious framework to provide spiritual justification for biological limitations and ensure workforce acceptance of mortality constraints."

[[CH06-P0390]] Religious framework. Spiritual justification. Workforce acceptance.

[[CH06-P0400]] The Church of Elom hadn't grown organically from the colonists' spiritual needs—it had been designed, planned, implemented alongside the genetic modifications. A tool created to make salves accept their shortened lives as divine gift rather than engineered limitation. Every prayer she had ever spoken, every service she had ever attended, every moment of comfort she had found in her faith—all of it had been manufactured. Designed. Part of the control system.

[[CH06-P0410]] Marnette's hands trembled as she turned the pages, scanning for more information, more details, more proof of what had been done to her people. The book was a technical manual of some kind, filled with charts and diagrams and terminology she didn't understand. Apoptosis triggers. Telomere degradation sequences. Cellular senescence protocols. Methylation markers. Genetic kill switches.

[[CH06-P0420]] The words meant nothing to her—she was a caregiver, not a geneticist—but their context was clear. This was the blueprint for how to make human beings die on schedule. This was the instruction manual for genocide spread across generations, for murder so slow and so systematic that the victims would never even know they were being killed.

[[CH06-P0430]] She set the book aside and searched for the folder Elara had mentioned. It took her twenty minutes of increasingly frantic searching, pulling out books and pushing aside data crystals, before she found it—a simple gray folder tucked behind a row of ancient texts, labeled in Frost's shaky handwriting: "MAYFLY - Complete."

[[CH06-P0440]] Inside were documents arranged in chronological order, each one a piece of the puzzle Frost had spent years assembling. The first was a reproduction of what appeared to be a founding document, dated to the colony's earliest years. The header read: "Proposal for Colony Workforce Optimization - Confidential - Council Members Only."

[[CH06-P0450]] Marnette sat down heavily on the nearest chair, her legs suddenly unable to support her, and began to read.

[[CH06-P0460]] ***

[[CH06-P0470]] The document was written in language that felt simultaneously familiar and alien—words she knew arranged in ways that made her stomach turn. It began with a summary of "challenges facing long-term colony viability," listing concerns about resource allocation, population growth, and what it called "social stability in isolated environment conditions."

[[CH06-P0480]] "Earth's destruction resulted directly from uncontrolled population expansion and competition for limited resources," the document stated in cold, matter-of-fact prose. "The Martian colony cannot repeat these errors. History has demonstrated that unchecked population growth leads inevitably to resource wars, social collapse, and extinction-level conflict. Sustainable civilization requires careful management of population dynamics, including lifespan parameters optimized for colony needs rather than individual preference."

[[CH06-P0490]] Optimized for colony needs. As if human lives were production metrics to be adjusted for efficiency. As if people were machines whose operational parameters could be tweaked for better performance.

[[CH06-P0500]] The proposal outlined three options for "workforce management." The first was traditional population control through birth limitations—deemed "insufficient and politically problematic, as reproductive restrictions have historically generated significant resistance and enforcement challenges." The second was resource rationing that would result in "natural attrition"—rejected as "cruel, destabilizing, and likely to generate visible suffering that would undermine social cohesion."

[[CH06-P0510]] The third was "genetic modification of designated workforce population to implement optimal lifespan parameters, combined with religious framework to ensure acceptance of biological limitations as divine rather than engineered."

[[CH06-P0520]] Marnette read the third option three times, each reading making her more certain that she understood correctly and more desperate to believe she didn't.

[[CH06-P0530]] "Genetic modification offers the most humane and efficient solution to long-term colony viability concerns," the document continued. "By implementing cellular degradation protocols that terminate biological function within a 50-60 year window, we achieve multiple objectives simultaneously:

[[CH06-P0540]] "1. Stable population levels maintained without visible enforcement mechanisms "2. Efficient resource utilization through predictable workforce turnover "3. Prevention of knowledge accumulation that might enable workforce resistance to necessary social structures "4. Elimination of costly end-of-life care for extended elderly populations "5. Maintenance of social hierarchy through clear differentiation between administrative and workforce classes"

[[CH06-P0550]] Prevention of knowledge accumulation. They had designed salves to die before they could learn enough to question the system. They had built mortality into her DNA specifically to keep her ignorant.

[[CH06-P0560]] The document went on to discuss implementation details with the detached precision of an engineering specification. How the modifications would be introduced to the first generation of Mars-born colonists through embryonic gene therapy. How the changes would be hereditary, passed down through generations without any need for continued intervention. How the "designated workforce" would be distinguished from the "administrative class" through genetic markers that also coded for radically different lifespans.

[[CH06-P0570]] Administrative class. That was the Masters. They weren't naturally long-lived—they had been engineered for longevity just as salves had been engineered for brevity. Two sides of the same manipulation, two classes created in a laboratory, one designed to rule for centuries and one designed to serve and die in decades.

[[CH06-P0580]] At the bottom of the document was a list of signatures—the colony council members who had voted for the proposal. Marnette recognized some of the names from history lessons: founders, heroes, the pioneers whose faces appeared on memorial displays throughout the colony. Visionaries, she had been taught. Saviors who had led humanity to its new home.

[[CH06-P0590]] And there, third from the bottom, written in careful handwriting that she recognized from labels throughout this very Archive: Timon Frost.

[[CH06-P0600]] He had signed this. He had voted for this. The man who had spent his final weeks trying to undo the harm had been one of its original architects. He had sat in a council chamber centuries ago and agreed that some humans should be designed to die young so that others could live forever.

[[CH06-P0610]] Forgive me, he had whispered at the end. Forgive us all.

[[CH06-P0620]] Now she understood what he was asking forgiveness for. Now she understood the weight he had carried for nearly eight hundred years, the guilt that had driven him to choose her, to plant seeds of doubt, to try desperately to confess before his body finally gave out.

[[CH06-P0630]] She didn't know if she could forgive him. She didn't know if forgiveness was even possible for something this enormous.

[[CH06-P0640]] ***

[[CH06-P0650]] The folder contained more documents—meeting minutes, research reports, implementation schedules, progress assessments. Marnette skimmed them with growing horror, catching phrases that burned themselves into her memory like brands:

[[CH06-P0660]] "Workforce units demonstrate optimal productivity during years 20-45, with declining efficiency thereafter justifying termination window placement. 50-year target lifespan maximizes labor extraction while minimizing resource expenditure on declining units."

[[CH06-P0670]] Units. They called salves units, like machines to be operated and discarded. Like equipment with a predetermined service life.

[[CH06-P0680]] "Religious framework integration proceeding ahead of schedule. Workforce acceptance of 'designated time' doctrine now at 94%, with resistance limited to outlier individuals who can be addressed through community pressure and, where necessary, isolation protocols."

[[CH06-P0690]] Outlier individuals. People like Thomas, who asked questions. People like her grandmother's generation, who might have remembered when things were different. People like her, now, who couldn't stop asking why.

[[CH06-P0700]] "Elon Protocol update: Founder's name successfully incorporated into religious mythology. 'Elom' designation provides divine authority for social structure while obscuring historical origins and preventing workforce investigation into founding documents."

[[CH06-P0710]] There it was, stated plainly in bureaucratic language. Elom was Elon. The god she had prayed to her entire life was a man—a man whose name had been deliberately corrupted, whose vision had been twisted, whose legacy had been transformed into a tool of control. Every time she had spoken that name in prayer, she had been participating in her own oppression without even knowing it.

[[CH06-P0720]] Marnette set down the document she was reading and pressed her hands against her eyes, trying to stop the tears that threatened to fall. She had known. Since Frost's death, she had suspected, had feared, had refused to fully believe. But seeing it written so clearly, so clinically, in documents signed by the founders she had been taught to revere—that was different. That was proof. That was the end of everything she had believed about herself, her purpose, her place in what she had thought was Elom's design.

[[CH06-P0730]] There was no Elom. There was no divine design. There was only engineering, control, manipulation that had lasted for seven centuries and would continue forever unless someone stopped it.

[[CH06-P0740]] She thought of her grandmother, dead at fifty-three from what they called cellular breakdown—what these documents called "successful completion of predetermined lifecycle parameters." She thought of Elder Maris, dead at fifty-eight, accepting her end with faith and grace, never knowing that her acceptance had been engineered as surely as her death. She thought of every salve she had ever cared for, every death she had witnessed, every prayer she had spoken over bodies that had been designed to fail on schedule.

[[CH06-P0750]] All of it. All of it was manufactured. Every death, every prayer, every acceptance of "designated time"—all of it was the intended result of decisions made by people who had viewed salves as resources to be managed rather than humans to be respected.

[[CH06-P0760]] Marnette pulled her hands away from her face and forced herself to continue reading. If she was going to carry this knowledge, she needed to understand it fully. She needed to know exactly what had been done to her people, and why, and by whom.

[[CH06-P0770]] The next document was titled "Church of Elom Establishment Protocols - Implementation Guide." She was reaching for it when she heard a sound from somewhere in the residence—a door opening, footsteps in the corridor, voices murmuring in conversation.

[[CH06-P0780]] ***

[[CH06-P0790]] Marnette froze, her hand still extended toward the document. The footsteps were getting closer, moving through the residence with the unhurried pace of people who belonged there. Multiple people—she could hear at least two distinct voices, maybe three. The maintenance staff, she realized with a jolt of fear. Elara had said they would arrive for daily inspection. How long had she been in the Archive? It felt like minutes, but the ache in her back and the stiffness in her legs suggested hours had passed while she was lost in the horror of what she was reading.

[[CH06-P0800]] She looked around frantically for a place to hide. The Archive had no doors other than the main entrance, no windows, no obvious escape route. The shelves were too closely packed to conceal a person, and the space beneath the tables was visible from the doorway. If they entered this room and looked around with any attention at all, they would find her.

[[CH06-P0810]] The voices grew louder, words becoming distinct as they approached.

[[CH06-P0820]] "—told you, Elara said she'd already done the morning check. We don't need to go through the whole place." A young male voice, impatient.

[[CH06-P0830]] "Protocol is protocol. The historical society wants documentation that the Archive is undisturbed until they can complete their assessment. That means visual confirmation, not Elara's word." An older voice, female, with the tone of someone accustomed to giving orders.

[[CH06-P0840]] They were coming to the Archive. They were specifically coming to check this room.

[[CH06-P0850]] Marnette grabbed the gray folder and stuffed it inside her tunic, pressing it flat against her stomach. The documents crinkled audibly, but the noise was lost in the sound of footsteps coming closer. She looked around desperately for anywhere to hide, any corner or alcove she might have missed.

[[CH06-P0860]] There—behind the largest shelf, in the narrow gap between the bookcase and the wall. It was barely wide enough for her body, filled with dust and shadows, but it was the only option she had.

[[CH06-P0870]] She squeezed into the gap just as the Archive door began to open, pressing herself against the wall, trying to control her breathing, trying to make herself invisible. Dust swirled around her, disturbed by her movement, and she clamped a hand over her nose to prevent the sneeze that threatened to betray her.

[[CH06-P0880]] "See?" the young voice said. "Nothing's changed. Same as yesterday, same as the day before."

[[CH06-P0890]] "We still need to document it." The older woman's footsteps moved into the room, circling slowly. "The historical society is very particular about these things. Master Frost's collection is supposed to be one of the most comprehensive in the colony. They're sending a full assessment team next month."

[[CH06-P0900]] Through a gap between books, Marnette could see fragments of the scene: gray maintenance coveralls, a datapad, the woman's lined face as she examined the room with professional attention. The woman was older, perhaps in her fifties—not much time left, Marnette thought with sudden bitter clarity. Not much time before her cells started their programmed shutdown.

[[CH06-P0910]] "Everything appears to be in order," the woman said, making notes on her datapad. "Books undisturbed, data crystals in place, environmental controls functioning within parameters." She moved to the center table, glanced at the brown leather book still sitting there, made another note. "Wait. This book is open. It should be closed."

[[CH06-P0920]] Marnette's heart stopped. She had left the book open when she moved to search for the folder. A small detail, but enough to raise questions.

[[CH06-P0930]] "Probably Elara looking through things," the young man said. "She was close to Master Frost. Makes sense she'd want to spend time with his things."

[[CH06-P0940]] The woman frowned but made a note rather than investigating further. "I'll mention it in the report. Could be nothing, could be someone's been in here who shouldn't be." She completed her circuit of the room and headed back toward the door. "Come on, we've got three more residences to check today."

[[CH06-P0950]] The footsteps receded, the voices fading as they moved away from the Archive, away from the secret Marnette carried pressed against her chest. She waited, counting her heartbeats, listening for any sign that they might return. One minute. Two. Five.

[[CH06-P0960]] Silence.

[[CH06-P0970]] She extracted herself from her hiding place, gasping for air, her body trembling with residual fear. The folder shifted against her stomach, its corners digging into her skin. She had to leave. She had to leave now, before the inspection team completed their rounds and returned, before Elara's explanation about the open book raised more questions.

[[CH06-P0980]] But first—she turned back to the table where the Church establishment document still lay. She couldn't take it; the folder was already bulging against her stomach, and any more would be obvious beneath her tunic. But she could read it. She could memorize what she needed.

[[CH06-P0990]] She grabbed the document and scanned it quickly, her eyes catching key phrases and burning them into her memory:

[[CH06-P1000]] "Church teachings will emphasize acceptance of biological limitations as divine gift rather than engineered constraint... Founder's name modification from 'Elon' to 'Elom' provides necessary historical distance and prevents workforce investigation into authentic founding records... Prayer language specifically designed to reinforce designated lifespan as sacred purpose, embedding acceptance at subconscious level... 'Designated time' doctrine critical for preventing workforce questioning of mortality parameters and maintaining social stability..."

[[CH06-P1010]] It was all there. The entire religious framework that had shaped her life, that had given her comfort and purpose and meaning—all of it was designed. Manufactured. A tool of control as surely as the genetic modifications that would kill her before she reached sixty. Every prayer she had ever spoken had been written by engineers, not prophets. Every moment of faith had been a moment of manipulation she hadn't known she was experiencing.

[[CH06-P1020]] Marnette set the document down and fled.

[[CH06-P1030]] ***

[[CH06-P1040]] The transport back to the salve district was crowded with workers returning from early shifts, their gray tunics identical to hers, their faces showing the same weariness of lives lived in service. Marnette sat among them with the folder hidden beneath her tunic, feeling like a bomb that might detonate at any moment.

[[CH06-P1050]] She looked at the faces around her—young and old, male and female, all of them carrying the same genetic modifications, all of them destined to die according to the same engineered schedule. A woman across from her, perhaps forty, laughed at something her companion said, her face showing the first signs of the aging that would accelerate over the next decade until her cells terminated. A young man, barely twenty, stared at nothing with the tired eyes of someone who had worked a long shift, not knowing that he had perhaps thirty or forty years left before his body shut down.

[[CH06-P1060]] They didn't know. None of them knew. They went about their days, worked their shifts, prayed their prayers, accepted their designated time with faith and grace—just as she had done, just as her grandmother had done, just as every salve had done for seven hundred years. They were living inside a lie so complete that the bars of their cage were invisible, so pervasive that the chains felt like comfort.

[[CH06-P1070]] And now she knew. Now she carried proof that everything they believed was false, that their lives had been designed and limited by people who viewed them as resources, that even their god was a manufactured fiction created to keep them docile.

[[CH06-P1080]] What was she supposed to do with this knowledge?

[[CH06-P1090]] Tell them, Frost had whispered. But how? Who would believe her? Who would even want to believe her? The truth was so enormous, so devastating, that most people would reject it simply to preserve their sanity, their faith, their ability to face each day without the crushing weight of what had been stolen from them.

[[CH06-P1100]] She thought of Lena, faithful Lena with her green-stained hands and her sincere prayers. How could Marnette tell her that Elom was a lie? That her grandmother's peaceful death had been the intended result of genetic engineering? That her entire understanding of purpose and meaning was a cage designed by people who had never considered her fully human?

[[CH06-P1110]] She thought of Thomas, already questioning, already doubting, already sensing that something was wrong. He was searching for the same truth she had now found. Would it help him to know? Would the knowledge give him peace, or would it destroy him? Would he be able to carry the weight that was now crushing her chest with every breath?

[[CH06-P1120]] She thought of Priest Malachi, fifty-seven years old, silver hair and lined face, decades of faithful service. He had devoted his entire life to teaching doctrines that had been written by engineers, not prophets. Did he know? Was he complicit, or was he as deceived as everyone else? Was his faith genuine or performed? And if she told him the truth, would he help her or turn her in?

[[CH06-P1130]] The questions spiraled through her mind as the transport carried her home, each one leading to more questions, none of them leading to answers. She had proof, but proof meant nothing if no one was willing to see it. She had truth, but truth was useless if speaking it destroyed her before she could share it.

[[CH06-P1140]] The transport stopped at the salve district platform, and Marnette walked through the familiar gray corridors with the weight of centuries pressing down on her shoulders. The memorial wall appeared on her left, names of the dead inscribed in neat rows, each one a life lived and ended according to the parameters set by people who had been dead for centuries. Her grandmother's name was there somewhere, among hundreds of others who had died on schedule, their cells terminating as designed, their faith intact to the end.

[[CH06-P1150]] She wanted to scream. She wanted to tear the wall down, to shout the truth until everyone heard, to shatter the comfortable lies that kept her people docile and accepting. But she couldn't. Not yet. Not until she understood more, planned more, found some way to make the truth mean something beyond her own horror.

[[CH06-P1160]] Her quarters were exactly as she had left them that morning—small, functional, clean. The sleeping pad, the narrow closet, the tiny window looking out at nothing. The two carvings of Elom on her bedside table, their benevolent eyes watching her enter with the evidence of their lie pressed against her chest.

[[CH06-P1170]] Marnette sat on the edge of her bed and pulled the folder from beneath her tunic. The documents spilled across her lap, each one a piece of evidence, each one a blow against everything she had ever believed.

[[CH06-P1180]] "Fifty years," she whispered, staring at the proposal that had condemned generations. "They gave us fifty years because it was convenient. Because it was efficient. Because they didn't want us to live long enough to figure out what they had done."

[[CH06-P1190]] She looked at her hands, at the skin that carried the genetic modifications written into her cells. Somewhere in her DNA were the sequences that would kill her—the apoptosis triggers, the degradation protocols, the kill switches that would activate in another twenty-five or thirty years. Her death was already written into her body, coded by people who had been dead for centuries. She would age, weaken, and terminate on schedule, just like her grandmother, just like Elder Maris, just like every salve who had ever lived.

[[CH06-P1200]] Unless.

[[CH06-P1210]] The thought came unbidden, dangerous, impossible. Unless someone changed it. Unless someone found a way to undo what had been done. Unless the truth, once spoken, sparked something that couldn't be controlled.

[[CH06-P1220]] Marnette looked at the documents in her lap, at the terrible proof of everything Frost had tried to tell her. She didn't know what to do with this knowledge. She didn't know who to tell or how to tell them. She didn't know if speaking the truth would change anything or simply destroy her.

[[CH06-P1230]] But she knew one thing with absolute certainty: she couldn't unknow this. She couldn't go back to the person she had been before she entered the Archive, before she read the words that had shattered her world. That person was gone, as completely as if her cells had terminated on schedule.

[[CH06-P1240]] What remained was someone new. Someone who knew the truth. Someone who carried proof that could change everything—if she could find the courage to use it, if she could find others willing to listen, if she could find a way forward that didn't end in her destruction.

[[CH06-P1250]] Marnette gathered the documents carefully, placing them back in the gray folder, hiding the folder beneath her sleeping pad where no one would think to look. Tomorrow she would decide what to do. Tomorrow she would figure out who to trust, who to tell, how to share this burden without being destroyed by it.

[[CH06-P1260]] But tonight, she sat in her small room with the weight of truth pressing down on her, and whispered the words that marked the end of one life and the beginning of another:

[[CH06-P1270]] "They made us this way. They chose this for us. And they lied about it for seven hundred years."

[[CH06-P1280]] The carving of Elom watched her from the bedside table, its benevolent eyes now seeming to mock everything she had ever believed. Marnette reached out and turned it to face the wall.

[[CH06-P1290]] She would never pray to that face again.
